sentences.text: Fred-- F-R-E-D-- Spiegel-- S-P-I-E-G-E-L.
Fritz Spiegel.
It's-- Fritz is F-R-I-T-Z.
Yeah, Dinslaken.
It's D-I-N-S-L-A-K-E-N.
That's in Germany.
It's in the Rhineland, in the Lower Rhine.
Yes, I do.
Yeah.
It was a three-story house.
And I remember-- I remember my mother was a widow, and she rented the upstairs and the downstairs.
I imagined this was in order to have money to-- to live on.
Upstairs was a non-Jewish couple-- retired policeman.
And downstairs was a Jewish family who was, somewhere along the line, all the way from Poland.
Why do I say that?
Because they disappeared.
In October '38, there was-- they took all the so-called Polish Jews and sent them-- put them across the border into Poland.
Now, the house that you lived in, you lived there with your mother?
My mother and my sister, my grandfather.
Of course, my father passed away in 1933.
I don't remember my father.
And so my grandfather, who had before that lived in his house with my uncle, moved in with us.
And for short while, we had-- she rent out a room to an aunt who, afterwards, got married-- sister-- her sister.
And then, the teacher at the Jewish school lived-- live for a while with us-- you know, like rented a room.
But that's-- it was a fairly large house, you know, when I-- when I think about it.
It's one of those European houses, you know, which have a lot of rooms-- you know, not that many bathrooms, but a lot of rooms.
In Germany, it's called [GERMAN].
I don't know the exact translation.
I don't think there is such a thing here, actually, anymore.
But it's-- to-- you sort of buy and sell cattle.
OK.
And in there in Dinslaken, as I found out many years later, that one of the large places where the-- how do you call it?
Sell-- not sell, but-- where, you know-- when-- when somebody, you know, tries and give prices.
What's it called?
Auction, yeah.
One of the largest.
I can never think of that word-- funny.
One of the largest auction in-- in the Rhineland.
It was in-- was in our area, you know, for cattle.
OK.
And it was a very good business, apparently, till 1933, at least.
He was in his 40s.
He died in '33, and he was born in-- he was about 43, 44.
Yes, he was a soldier in the First World War.
He was a soldier already before the war started, and because in Germany, there was-- you had to have-- you know, there was-- you had to go into the service.
And he became a sergeant, and he fought in France.
He was wounded in 1917.
I think it was in the Battle of Verdun.
And was sent-- then he was sent back to Germany to recuperate.
And afterwards, he even got a pension because of his wounds.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he got several decorations.
I don't have most of them.
I only have one left.
But he got, you know, the famous Iron Crosses, which the Germans always use in every war.
He got at least three or four.
I only found one.
My mother used to keep them, I don't know what happened to them, but I found one when-- after she passed away.
He [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was in business together with my uncle, Uncle Adolf.
That's his brother-- older brother.
And then, when he passed away in 1933, my mother sort of was a partner in the business, even though she was not active, obviously.
She had two little children.
I have a sister who was four years older than I am.
But also, after Hitler got to power, the Jews were disenfranchised.
So the business-- and after a while, there were boycotts and whatever, so business wasn't that good anymore.
And then my uncle had to leave in 1936.
He left for Holland because he had been doing some-- he had been trying to help some Jews to leave Germany with-- with money, you know, because the Jews could leave Germany anytime they wanted to.
The problem was they couldn't take the capital with them.
They couldn't really, but he made some sort of arrangement through other people, and they did.
And apparently, this was not legal.
So eventually, they caught up with him.
And he-- I remember him leaving.
I was only four years old.
I remember he left overnight, so to speak.
I remember one-- wake up in the morning, he was gone with his family.
No, no.
She grew up in a town called Petershagen.
It isn't that far away.
I guess, in those days, it was further than it is today.
Not that it moved closer, it's just that it's easier to get there.
It's-- and it's in the same general area, I would say.
You know.
It's further north-- northeast of where we live.
In 1927, and my sister was born in 1928.
Edith.
That's the same name she had in German, Edith.
So she always kept the same name.
And then, I was born in 1932, and my name, as I said originally, was Fritz.
I would say traditional household.
We were, traditionally, religious.
We're not Orthodox like the Hasidim.
But we used-- I think it was kosher.
It probably was.
But then, later on, you couldn't keep kosher because the-- the-- it was forbidden.
The ritual slaughter was forbidden in Germany after Hitler came to power.
So I-- I don't think that-- by the time I remember well, I don't think it was kosher.
Couldn't have been because it wasn't-- you could no longer buy kosher meat or anything.
Vaguely Passover.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember-- I also remember, vaguely, going to the synagogue, but I don't remember that well.
We-- we had-- we had in the town, an orphanage where I used to go to a lot.
This orphanage, really, was a Jewish orphanage, which was run-- it was for the whole Rhineland, for the whole area.
And it was run by Dr.
Leopold Rothschild, who was a well-known educator and very respected man.
And even though I wasn't-- I was like a half an orphan.
That's not-- that's not the reason I went there.
The reason was, it was a safe place to go to.
It was a big house with-- with fence-- with a fence around it.
It had a back to play.
You could go and play there without being bothered by the-- by other kids.
You know.
I remember that in-- I used to go with my grandfather.
Behind the house there was a park.
And we used to-- he used to take me for a walk there.
But the older kids used to throw stones at us and call us dirty Jew, and-- and-- and the adults used to turn the other way or help them.
So it wasn't really a very nice place to go to anymore, the park.
It was a very nice park, but you couldn't really-- wasn't a good place for a young boy, anyway-- for a little boy.
I remember playing with other kids, and-- and one of the things I remember, I guess, because it was scary-- one of the things they-- they started to-- to do is dig trenches.
I guess, this was under the influence of the adults.
Maybe they were preparing for war-- dig trenches and then, sit in them.

So I remember I played with them, and-- and I sat in the trench, and they started to throw the sand on me from-- you know, from outside till one of the mothers from the other kids came and pulled me out.
You know.
So I-- I used to go and play in the-- in the orphanage where I remember, sort of vaguely, one of the girls.
She must been 13, 14 years old at the time.
So she used to watch me like a baby-- she was like a babysitter.
OK.
An-- and she-- of course, they had swings and things like that.
And I guess, I was frightened.
Now, the towns are quite small?
Yes, in those days it had, maybe, 20,000 inhabitants, but it was a very strong Jewish community-- small, but strong, very nice synagogue.
And like I said, it was one of the reasons it was so well-- so strong-- was because of the orphanage.
I think that helped to sort of make it a strong community.
And also, this Dr.
Rothschild was Orthodox-- you know, modern Orthodox, also.
And he was a very strong individual and had a lot of influence.
I don't even remember whether there was a rabbi or not in-- in the community.
I know there was a teacher.
I don't remember if there was.
I don't remember him.
But I know that a lot of things he did, and he was, more or less, the leader of the community in many ways.
Yes.
As soon as I could remember, you know.
Like I said, I couldn't go out and play.
I played with other kids, but it was-- after a while, it became dangerous.
You know.
And then, of course, when the time came to go to school-- to kindergarten-- go to school, we couldn't anymore because Jewish kids could not go to the same school as-- as other kids.
But they had established a Jewish school, which I went to.
OK.
Now, I-- I guess the community must have been big enough to support-- it was a small school, but it-- it-- it supported a school.
And I don't know whether-- I'm sure they got a few Jewish families in little villages in the area.
They probably came there, too, I imagine.
But I remember the name of the teacher, even.
You know, there was a teacher.
He lived-- he was the gentleman who lived-- who rented a room from my mother.
And he taught.
He was the teacher there.
I think there's only one teacher.
I don't remember anybody else.
Might have been teacher's aids, but I don't-- don't remember them.
But I remember the-- the orphanage much better than I remember the school, OK, because it sort of, I guess, left more of an impression on me, being organized.
And-- and-- and it was, you know, [? even ?] [? nice ?] to go to [? there ?] [? now. ?] Also, all the-- now that I remember, when I think about it, the Jewish holidays.
You asked me before about the Jewish holidays.
We used to go there, actually, now that I think about it.
You know.
I remember having one Purim there, and they had a play, you know, on the stage where the older-- older orphanage people, the members, performed.
It was very good.
And-- and Passover-- and I remember now, come to think of it, that's where we used to go because I don't-- I didn't remember anything in the house, really, but I do remember going to the orphanage.
And that's-- that's where we all-- everybody went.
Everybody went there later on, you know, because that was like the central place, you know, for everybody to get together.
And it was a fairly sizable place.
There probably were.
But I-- I remember something, but I don't really remember, you know, because it went sort of by me, you know.
It was adults talking to each other, and-- and they sort of try to shield you from that.
You know.
So I don't really know-- I know that some of the older-- older boys-- the orphanage was to about, I think, age 16 or 17 or 18.
They-- they had problems.
They were beaten up sometimes, you know, if they caught them on their own.
They had to go with a few of them together, always.
This was always the-- you had to make sure that nobody gets caught on his own.
But really, actual incident, I don't remember.
I don't remember.
Till-- till the Kristallnacht, of course, which we'll mention in-- want me to mention the Kristallnacht?
Well, we'll get to that shortly.
How-- how did your family-- or did they try to explain to you what was going on?
I don't know because, really-- the-- I'll tell you who tried to explain to me, strangely enough.
The people upstairs were not Jewish.
You know.
He was of a retired policeman, and his wife, they were grandparent, but they-- the children [? can't ?] live somewhere else.
And I used to go there a lot, also.
Because he used to tell me stories about the First World War, which I don't know why I found it interesting, but I guess I found it interesting.
And he tried to explain to me what was happening.
You know.
He said, because you're Jewish, you know, you have these problems.
But he didn't really explain it that well.
Why?
I guess, he didn't think that a child of my age would understand it anyway.
You know.
But he tried to explain that it wasn't-- he didn't feel that it was right.
You know, it was wrong.
He was against this type of demonstrations and-- and beatings and whatever-- what was happening.
Don't forget, it was a small town.
Everybody knew-- knew everybody, so they were careful.
You know.
Usually, they were out-- they brought in outsiders.
The Nazis brought outsiders to do the bad things because the-- many years, everybody had lived together and worked together.
And everybody knew my father.
Even so he had died in the meanwhile, but they all knew my grandfather.
My grandfather used to play cards.
He was a card player.
You know, and the German-- there's a Germany card game called skat, which I don't know how it goes.
You know.
I have no-- I don't have a clue.
But I know my uncle used to play down-- even when I-- when I saw him years later, and he used to play, and he was well-known for that.
He used to play a lot of friends, and-- and the whole town knew him.
And they knew my father well because he had served in-- in the First World War, and they had a lot of friends.
And-- but then, slowly but surely, I guess some of them started to distance themselves.
But I don't think the-- the town-- the people that lived there for a while in the town, [? well, ?] they were not active in-- in-- in any of the early incidents, at least.
You know.
I know that, later on, it got worse, you know, but at the beginning, I guess, friendship from way past still meant something.
Even ?] I remember that when-- one day when I went for a walk with my grandfather, some so-called friends of his were no better.
They started calling-- calling him dirty Jew and-- and-- and all this.
And these were people who we had known for many years.
I was frightened, you know, because, I think, my grandfather was in shock.
And he didn't-- of all people, didn't expect it from them.
OK.
So he-- we were sitting on a bench, so we got up and we left and went back home.
But in general, you know, when you're-- I was four or five years old.
You don't-- you remember only sort of outstanding incidents, you know, things which sort of left-- like-- like I said, when they were sitting in the trench, and they started to throw sand on me.
That I remember because it was frightening.
You know.
And we also remember most of the time, going to the orphanage for anything-- you know, any social, whatever you want to call it, life.
OK.
Well, Kristallnacht was, of course, an event which I was six and a half at the time.
It's November 9-- 10, 1938.
Can't forget.
You know, you can't forget, even at that age, already.
It started off, strangely enough-- it start-- I remember the-- the-- going upstairs early in the morning to see this-- I called them friends, you know, these elderly people.
And while I was upstairs, I could see the synagogue burning.
And the synagogue was burning-- because the synagogue wasn't that far away-- and I was wondering what was happening.
And he really-- really didn't-- didn't want to explain to me.
I think he was as frightened as everybody else.
And then, I hear the noise, and then, the-- some hooligans.
This is what they were, but I mean, they were normal people.
What I-- you know, I guess, in that stage, they usually were half-drunk before they came in.
They came in with axes and-- and whatever and came into the house-- into our part of the house-- OK, walk in and smashed everything.
And I remember my mother and my sister screaming on the balcony.
And there was an aunt visiting us, a sister of my father.
She was, also, outside screaming on the balcony.
And they started smashing everything.
And--
Why do they call it a Kristallnacht?
It's because they used to-- they threw everything out into the streets.
That's what they did-- you know, all-- all the glasses and-- and-- and whatever else was threw out into the street, and it was smashed.
And they continued to, literally, hack everything to pieces, you know, into firewood.
And then, they wanted to light a house-- you know, put the house on fire.
And then they-- then my friend upstairs came down, and said, wait a minute.
This is my house.
You can't just set this house on fire.
And then they left.
OK.
They left.
But then, we had to leave, too.
They came and picked us up.
The police.
Police-- I think they were police.
It could've been army people.
I think they were police with rifles at the ready.
And they took us.
We couldn't stay in the house.
They took us to the Jewish school where they put straw on the floor where we could lie down.
We were not allowed to go back home then.
And we stayed there overnight.
I remember my mother wake me up in the middle of the night to tell me that my babysitter had come to say goodbye.
You know, she was living in the orphanage, and the orphanage children were leaving.
I found out many years later where they went, but at the time, of course, I didn't know where they were going, but they were leaving.
Her name was Francesca, I remember.
And-- so she came to say goodbye.
She woke me up, and of course, I never saw her again.
Yes.
They went first to Cologne.
You know.
From there, they went to Belgium.
And there-- at the time of the Kristallnacht, there were 28 kids left in the orphanage.
Of those, 27 were killed.
Only one survived.
Yeah.
No, they-- no, they were killed, afterwards, in Auschwitz.
They all-- they all escaped to Belgium-- some to Holland, some to Belgium.
And then, when after the German invasion, they all-- all got picked up and just one managed to-- to survive.
And some older kids survived who were not at the orphanage.
See what happened, those over 16 or 17 had left.
Many of them had left the orphanage because of the situation and had gone out on their own.
Some of them already had gone to Holland.
Some of them had gone to France.
One particular one, who I didn't know, of course-- I found out, many years later, about him.
He became a rabbi.
He-- he had gone east.
He had just finished studying to be a rabbi.
And he went-- he finished up in Shanghai.
You know, some German Jews finished up in Shanghai.
He was amongst those, and he, now, lives in California.
But I do know that the orphanage children were treated terribly.
This is-- again, I-- I don't really remember it that well.

I-- I remembered some very vaguely, but this was explained to me many years later when I visited a town, that they took them-- they took the younger kids and put them on the rack-- you know these rack wagons, like where you put straw on-- and-- and put them on that and make the bigger kids pull the wagon through the streets.
And then, they were-- the-- the-- the community was standing there watching and while they-- while they were spit at and beaten up.
And this-- this was done several times-- make them go through the main street and let everybody spit on them and then, sort of tried to abuse them.
OK.
This is what happened to the--
The-- it's-- it's-- there's a written record of it.
Of course, the head of the orphanage, Rothschild, had left.
He had gone to Palestine.
He had left.
I don't know exactly when.
I think in the beginning of '38, and he left somebody else in charge who had been there, a-- a gentleman called Yitzhak Hirsch, who was a-- who was a journalist.
He was also educator, also was a journalist.

So he kept-- sort of as a-- as a good journalist, he kept sort of a blow by blow account of what was happening-- like an eyewitness account, you know, which is always the best because, afterwards, years later, you remember but you don't really remember the things exactly the way they were.
And he made this part of a book which he published.
He published about his own hometown.
He wasn't from Dinslaken.
He was from somewhere else, but this-- the chapter was devoted to the Kristallnacht in Dinslaken.
And then, the town of Dinslaken itself, the elders went to-- some years ago-- went to visit him in Israel and bought this-- bought this from him.
You know, they gave him-- they wanted to because he wasn't doing well, so they gave him money for it.

And they published it and to show the people what-- how bad they had been, you know, and how dangerous it is to-- to-- like-- like people always say the worst part of it was people stand by and watch.
You know, they don't-- they don't do anything about it.
You know, they just-- they're bystanders.
OK.
And this was the majority of people who were bystanders, and some of them were very active, you know, in-- in helping the Nazis.
Not really, you know, because the thing is, I can't judge what happened in '33 and '34 because I was too small.
But I was surprised at the Kristallnacht because it had been violent, but not-- nothing-- nothing special-- you know, nothing more than had been the day before the day before the day before.
You see?
So Kristallnacht was a-- it was a program.
It's-- it's-- it's a fully-fledged program.
It was done by the government.
It was a government-organized program.
I mean, people were actually-- you know, the SS and SA, they were actually organized by the government and-- and had faculties to come and do this.
You know, it wasn't left to the local populace like these so-called-- so-called popular programs, which you read about in Poland, which, also, were organized, but different.
It was organized locally.
This was, actually, a government order, you know, basically.
And-- and I guess, people were taken by surprise because this was done-- it was prepared like overnight, or I imagine.
You know.
I mean, I don't know the details, but it was very violent.
Of course, a lot of men over the age of 17, I think, were taken to concentration camps-- you know, to Dachau and Buchenwald.
My grandfather was taken away.
That I remember.
He was beaten up and was taken away.
Yeah, the same day, not the next day--
Yeah, yeah, I didn't see him.
That same day, I didn't see him.
And-- but I saw him again, afterwards, in Holland.
He came to Holland afterwards.
OK.
He was let go because of his age.

And also, I-- I think, he was let go because always someone along the line-- in those days, especially, there always was an old soldier who would remember and say to himself, how can I do this to this friend of mine who-- who was with me?
Because my grandfather, also, served in the army.

He might not have been in-- I don't think he was in the First World War anymore, but he was in the German Army like everybody else, and he had a lot of people he knew who had been with him in the army.
So he was let go after awhile.
OK.
I don't know whether he was in Dachau or Buchenwald.
I don't know because I have no way of even checking that out.
There's no way I can do it.
But this was still at about the end of 1938--
Yeah, yeah.
He was taken that same day.
When we were sent to the school, he was taken to-- into prison first, OK, and then-- all the men were taken to prison.
OK.
And-- and they used to say in the books, they say, I think, between 17 and 45.
I mean, he was above 45, and yet, he was taken away.
He wasn't the only one.
He was not an exception.
You know.
But he was let go earlier, I guess, maybe because of his age or because of-- maybe somewhere along the lines, after a little while, how-- you know, let-- let him go.
You know, what did he do to us?
OK.
Were you allowed to take anything with you when you went to-- were taken to the school?
I think we took some blankets.
Yeah, blankets and-- and maybe a change of clothes.
Maybe my mother took something.
But, really, we weren't allowed to-- to sort of gather our belongings.
But later on, we were.
You know.
That came later.
A few days later, we were sent to Holland.
My mother wanted to find a solution.
You know.
She knew-- then she knew we couldn't stay in Germany.
I guess my mother hoped that she could stay in Germany and that the situation would just go away.
Like many-- many German-Jews thought-- felt this way-- you know, this is only a passing thing, and we are Germans of the Jewish faith.
You know, that's-- that's how they looked upon themselves.
They felt themselves more German than Jewish.
Many of the German Jews were like that.
I mean, my-- my-- from both my father and my mother's side, it goes back many generations.

I mean, I tried to check out-- when I was in Dinslaken-- on my father's side, there's no way of going-- finding [? that ?] [? today. ?] What I could find from the grandfather and the great-grandfather all were from the same area in Germany.
OK.
So they felt themselves very German and, then, Jewish-- you know, of the Jewish faith.
And so it wasn't-- it was, also, shock for them.
Many-- many German Jews left, you know.
But those who remained behind, by the time-- after the Kristallnacht, it became a panic to-- to leave, in a way.
And you couldn't.
You know.
You couldn't leave because nobody was accepting you.
OK, we have to stop.
Did we do OK?
